items.promptText: Which sentence starts with a fronted adverbial?
visibleOptionsOrRows: In the morning, the market opens early.
The market opens early in the morning.
Could the market open early?
The market opens and traders hurry in.
solutionLines: A fronted adverbial gives when, where or how information at the start of the sentence.
In KS2 contexts it is commonly followed by a comma.

items.promptText: Which sentence starts with a fronted adverbial?
visibleOptionsOrRows: She is feeling tired, so Kal is going to her room.
After dinner, Kal is going to her room.
Arun told me that Kal is going to her room.
I wonder when Kal is going to her room.
solutionLines: A fronted adverbial gives when, where or how information at the start of the sentence.
In KS2 contexts it is commonly followed by a comma.

items.promptText: Which option is the subordinate clause in the sentence below?When the bell rang, the pupils lined up quietly.
visibleOptionsOrRows: the pupils lined up quietly
When the bell rang
lined up quietly
the bell
solutionLines: A subordinate clause often begins with a conjunction such as because, when, if or although.
It depends on the main clause to complete the full meaning.

icy wear
solutionLines: A subordinate clause often begins with a conjunction such as because, when, if or although.

items.promptText: Which sentence contains a relative clause?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The book that I borrowed was brilliant.
After I borrowed the book, it was brilliant.
I borrowed the book and it was brilliant.
Borrowing the book, I found it brilliant.
solutionLines: A relative clause gives extra information about a noun.
It often begins with who, which, that, where, when or whose.

visibleOptionsOrRows: The village, which sits by the sea, is very quiet.

solutionLines: A relative clause gives extra information about a noun.
It often begins with who, which, that, where, when or whose.

items.promptText: Which version is clearest and avoids awkward repetition without becoming confusing?
visibleOptionsOrRows: Arjun gave Arjun's book to Arjun's sister because Arjun had finished Arjun's book.
Arjun gave his book to his sister because he had finished it.
Arjun gave his book to his sister because it had finished him.
He gave it to her because he had finished her.
solutionLines: Good cohesion reduces repetition without making the meaning unclear.
A pronoun must clearly point to the right noun.

She put it on the chair before she zipped it.
solutionLines: Good cohesion reduces repetition without making the meaning unclear.
A pronoun must clearly point to the right noun.

items.promptText: Insert a pair of brackets in the correct place.My cousin the youngest in the family won the chess trophy.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: My cousin (the youngest in the family) won the chess trophy.
solutionLines: The bracketed words add extra information about ‘my cousin’.
The main sentence still works without them.
The correct sentence is: My cousin (the youngest in the family) won the chess trophy.

items.promptText: Insert a pair of brackets in the correct place.Our class visited a castle the oldest in the county to help with our history project.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Our class visited a castle (the oldest in the county) to help with our history project.
solutionLines: Find the extra information that could be lifted out.
Place the brackets around that extra information.
The correct sentence is: Our class visited a castle (the oldest in the county) to help with our history project.

solutionLines: The bracketed words add extra information about ‘my cousin’.
The main sentence still works without them.
The correct sentence is: My cousin (the youngest in the family) won the chess trophy.

The correct sentence is: Our class visited a castle (the oldest in the county) to help with our history project.

items.promptText: Punctuate the direct speech correctly.Dad shouted “Run inside!”
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Dad shouted, “Run inside!”
solutionLines: A comma is needed before the direct speech after the reporting clause.
The exclamation mark stays inside the speech marks.
A correct answer is: Dad shouted, “Run inside!”

items.promptText: Punctuate the direct speech correctly.“Sit down” said the coach.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: “Sit down!” said the coach.
solutionLines: An exclamation mark is needed inside the speech marks to show the command.
No comma is needed after the speech marks because the speech ends with an exclamation mark.
A correct answer is: “Sit down!” said the coach.

items.promptText: Punctuate the direct speech correctly.“Where are you going” asked Mum.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: “Where are you going?” asked Mum.
solutionLines: The spoken words are a question.
The question mark belongs inside the speech marks.
A correct answer is: “Where are you going?” asked Mum.

items.promptText: Why is ‘I done my homework’ wrong in Standard English?
visibleOptionsOrRows: Because Standard English uses ‘did’, not ‘done’, in that sentence.
Because ‘homework’ cannot be the object.
Because the sentence should be passive.
Because all past tense verbs end in -ed.
solutionLines: Focus on the grammar reason, not just a vague comment that it ‘sounds better’.
The best explanation names the right feature and why it matters.

items.promptText: Why is there a comma after the opening words in this sentence?Before sunrise, the campers packed their bags.
visibleOptionsOrRows: Because the opening words are a fronted adverbial.
Because every long sentence needs a comma near the start.
Because ‘sunrise’ is a noun.
Because the sentence is in the past tense.
solutionLines: Focus on the grammar reason, not just a vague comment that it ‘sounds better’.
The best explanation names the right feature and why it matters.

items.promptText: Which punctuation mark best completes the sentence below?The rain stopped ___ the playground began to fill.
solutionLines: Both parts are complete clauses and closely linked in meaning.
A semi-colon can join closely related main clauses without a conjunction.
The best answer is: The rain stopped; the playground began to fill.

items.promptText: Which sentence is written in the passive voice?
visibleOptionsOrRows: Before assembly, the caretaker locked the hall.
The caretaker locked the hall before assembly.
The hall was locked by the caretaker before assembly.
The caretaker was locking the hall before assembly.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hall was locked by the caretaker before assembly.
solutionLines: In passive voice, the thing affected often comes before the doer.
The hall receives the action, so it comes first in the passive sentence.
The passive sentence is: The hall was locked by the caretaker before assembly.

items.promptText: Which sentence is written in the passive voice?

Aisha was painting the scenery for the play.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The scenery was painted by Aisha for the play.
solutionLines: In passive voice, the thing affected often comes before the doer.
The passive voice focuses on the scenery rather than the person doing the painting.
The passive sentence is: The scenery was painted by Aisha for the play.

items.promptText: In this sentence, what does the modal verb might show?The clouds are dark, so it might rain later.
visibleOptionsOrRows: It names the person doing the action.
It shows a fixed rule.
It shows possibility, not certainty.
It shows the action is happening now.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: It shows possibility, not certainty.
solutionLines: Modal verbs show meanings such as possibility, certainty, obligation, permission, or advice.
'Might' shows that rain is possible but not certain.

items.promptText: In this sentence, what does the modal verb should show?You should check your work before handing it in.
visibleOptionsOrRows: It shows something is impossible.
It turns the verb into the past tense.
It gives advice.
It marks direct speech.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: It gives advice.
solutionLines: Modal verbs show meanings such as possibility, certainty, obligation, permission, or advice.
'Should' commonly gives advice or a recommendation.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in small-animal hospital rather than small animal hospital?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The hyphen shows a missing letter.
The hyphen shows that the hospital building is small.
The hyphen shows that the hospital is for small animals.
The hyphen marks a fronted adverbial.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hyphen shows that the hospital is for small animals.
solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
The hyphen joins 'small' and 'animal' so they work together before 'hospital'.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in last-minute poster rather than last minute poster?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The hyphen shows that the poster is the final minute.
The hyphen marks direct speech.
The hyphen shows that 'last-minute' is one describing idea before 'poster'.
The hyphen joins two complete clauses.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hyphen shows that 'last-minute' is one describing idea before 'poster'.
solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
The compound adjective comes before the noun, so the hyphen protects the intended meaning.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in well-known author rather than well known author?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The hyphen shows the author owns a well.
The hyphen separates two independent clauses.
The hyphen shows that 'well-known' is one describing idea before 'author'.
The hyphen turns well into a noun.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hyphen shows that 'well-known' is one describing idea before 'author'.
solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
Compound adjectives before a noun use a hyphen to show they form a single modifier.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in twenty-four children rather than twenty four children?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The hyphen separates two different groups of children.
The hyphen shows possession by the number.
The hyphen links twenty and four as a single compound number.
The hyphen marks a question.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hyphen links twenty and four as a single compound number.
solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
Compound numbers from twenty-one to ninety-nine use a hyphen.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in re-cover the book rather than recover the book?
visibleOptionsOrRows: The hyphen shows that the book belongs to someone.
The hyphen shows cover again, not get back.
The hyphen means the same as a comma here.
The hyphen marks a subordinate clause.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The hyphen shows cover again, not get back.
solutionLines: A hyphen can join words so they work as one describing idea before a noun.
A hyphen after 're' can prevent confusion with a different word.

items.promptText: Why does the hyphen matter in old-fashioned clock rather than old fashioned clock?

items.promptText: Which option correctly joins a fronted adverbial to a main clause AND uses the right boundary punctuation?before the bell rang / the children lined up quietly
visibleOptionsOrRows: Before the bell rang; the children lined up quietly.
Before the bell rang the children lined up quietly.
Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.
Before, the bell rang the children lined up quietly.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.
solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
A fronted adverbial clause needs a comma after it to mark the boundary between the adverbial and the main clause. A semicolon is wrong because the adverbial is not an independent clause.
The correct option is: Before the bell rang, the children lined up quietly.

items.promptText: Which option correctly punctuates the sentence with a fronted adverbial AND identifies the main clause boundary?after the storm cleared / we inspected the damage
visibleOptionsOrRows: After the storm cleared we inspected the damage.
After the storm cleared: we inspected the damage.
After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.
After the storm, cleared we inspected the damage.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.
solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
The adverbial 'After the storm cleared' needs a comma to show where the subordinate clause ends and the main clause begins. A colon is not used to join an adverbial to a main clause.
The correct option is: After the storm cleared, we inspected the damage.

items.promptText: Which option places the comma correctly when the adverbial clause appears at the front?although the path was icy / nobody slipped
visibleOptionsOrRows: Although the path was icy nobody slipped.
Although the path was icy; nobody slipped.
Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.
Although, the path was icy nobody slipped.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.
solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
'Although the path was icy' is a subordinate adverbial clause showing concession. It needs a comma after it because it is fronted. A semicolon would wrongly treat it as an independent clause.
The correct option is: Although the path was icy, nobody slipped.

solutionLines: This question tests adverbials and clauses and boundary punctuation together.
A comma marks the fronted adverbial boundary, and a semicolon correctly separates the two independent clauses that follow. Using only commas creates a comma splice.
The correct option is: During the assembly, the head announced sports day; parents are invited.

items.promptText: Which option uses the correct tense, an appropriate modal verb, AND Standard English?A letter to parents about a school trip that will happen next week.

items.promptText: Which option replaces the repeated noun with a pronoun AND maintains formal register?The council has approved the plans. The council will begin work in September.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The council has approved the plans. Them lot will begin work in September.
The council has approved the plans. They're gonna start work in September.

The council has approved the plans. The council will begin work in September.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The council has approved the plans. It will begin work in September.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'It' avoids repetition of 'the council' (cohesion) while keeping the formal tone. 'They're gonna' is too informal. Repeating the noun weakens cohesion. 'Them lot' is non-standard.
The correct option is: The council has approved the plans. It will begin work in September.

solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
Joining the clauses avoids repeating 'The museum' (cohesion) and keeps formal vocabulary ('closes', 'must book'). 'Gotta' and 'You lot' are informal.
The correct option is: The museum opens at nine and closes at five. Visitors must book online.

It opens at nine and shuts at five. People gotta book online.
The museum opens at nine. It shuts at five. Visitors have gotta book online.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The museum opens at nine and closes at five. Visitors must book online.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
Joining the clauses avoids repeating 'The museum' (cohesion) and keeps formal vocabulary ('closes', 'must book'). 'Gotta' and 'You lot' are informal.
The correct option is: The museum opens at nine and closes at five. Visitors must book online.

items.promptText: Which option uses a pronoun for cohesion AND keeps formal vocabulary?Dr Patel presented the findings. Dr Patel explained the next steps.
visibleOptionsOrRows: Dr Patel presented the findings. She then chatted about what's next.
Dr Patel presented the findings. Dr Patel explained the next steps.
Patel presented the findings. She banged on about what comes next.
Dr Patel presented the findings. She then explained the next steps.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Dr Patel presented the findings. She then explained the next steps.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'She then explained' uses a pronoun to avoid repetition (cohesion) while maintaining formal language. 'Chatted about' and 'banged on' are informal.
The correct option is: Dr Patel presented the findings. She then explained the next steps.

items.promptText: Which option links the ideas cohesively AND keeps a formal tone?The project was completed on time. The project received praise from the governors.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The project was completed on time and received praise from the governors.
The project was completed on time. The project received praise from the governors.
The project was done on time and the governors were well chuffed.
It got done on time and the governors loved it.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The project was completed on time and received praise from the governors.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
Joining with 'and' avoids repetition (cohesion). 'Completed' and 'received praise' maintain formality. 'Well chuffed', 'got done', and 'loved it' are informal.
The correct option is: The project was completed on time and received praise from the governors.

items.promptText: Which option improves pronoun cohesion AND keeps the tone suitable for a school report?James has improved his reading. James now reads chapter books independently.
visibleOptionsOrRows: James has improved his reading. James now reads chapter books independently.
James has improved his reading. He now reads chapter books independently.
He's got better at reading. He reads chapter books by himself now.
James has improved his reading. The lad now reads chapter books on his own.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: James has improved his reading. He now reads chapter books independently.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'He' replaces the repeated name (cohesion) and the sentence stays formal. 'The lad' is too informal for a report. 'Got better' reduces formality.
The correct option is: James has improved his reading. He now reads chapter books independently.

items.promptText: Which option removes repetition AND maintains formal register?The charity raised three thousand pounds. The charity will donate it to the hospital.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The charity got three grand and is giving it to the hospital.
They raised three thousand pounds and they're gonna give it to the hospital.
The charity raised three thousand pounds, which it will donate to the hospital.
The charity raised three thousand pounds. The charity will donate it to the hospital.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The charity raised three thousand pounds, which it will donate to the hospital.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.

Using 'which it will' avoids repetition (cohesion) with a relative clause and maintains the formal register. 'Three grand', 'gonna', and 'They' without a clear referent weaken both cohesion and formality.
The correct option is: The charity raised three thousand pounds, which it will donate to the hospital.

items.promptText: Which option uses a pronoun to improve flow AND keeps formal language?The headteacher addressed the assembly. The headteacher reminded pupils about uniform.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The headteacher addressed the assembly. The headteacher reminded pupils about uniform.

The headteacher addressed the assembly. She reminded pupils about uniform expectations.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The headteacher addressed the assembly. She reminded pupils about uniform expectations.
solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'She reminded' uses a pronoun for cohesion and keeps the formal vocabulary. 'Had a go at' and 'told kids to sort' are too informal for this context.
The correct option is: The headteacher addressed the assembly. She reminded pupils about uniform expectations.

items.promptText: Which option avoids repetition through a pronoun AND uses formal phrasing?The experiment was successful. The experiment will be repeated next term.

solutionLines: This question tests pronouns cohesion and formality together.
'It will be repeated' uses a pronoun to avoid repetition (cohesion) and keeps the passive, formal style of scientific writing. 'Gonna' and 'have another go' are informal.
The correct option is: The experiment was successful. It will be repeated next term.

items.promptText: Identify the voice and the grammatical role of the underlined noun phrase.The trophy was awarded to Amir by the head teacher.
visibleOptionsOrRows: Voice of the sentence
Role of 'the trophy'
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Voice of the sentence: passive | Role of 'the trophy': subject
solutionLines: This question tests active passive and subject object together.
The sentence is passive because the action is done TO the trophy. In a passive sentence, the thing affected ('the trophy') becomes the grammatical subject.
Voice of the sentence: passive | Role of 'the trophy': subject

items.promptText: Identify the voice and the grammatical role of the underlined noun phrase.The goalkeeper saved the penalty brilliantly.
visibleOptionsOrRows: Voice of the sentence
Role of 'the penalty'
items.expectedAnswerSummary: Voice of the sentence: active | Role of 'the penalty': object
solutionLines: This question tests active passive and subject object together.
The sentence is active because the doer ('the goalkeeper') comes first and does the action. 'The penalty' receives the action, so it is the object.
Voice of the sentence: active | Role of 'the penalty': object

items.promptText: Which option correctly uses both the possessive apostrophe AND a hyphen to avoid ambiguity?The well known author's latest book topped the charts.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The well-known authors' latest book topped the charts.
The well known authors latest book topped the charts.
The well-known author's latest book topped the charts.
The well known author's latest book topped the charts.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The well-known author's latest book topped the charts.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
A hyphen in 'well-known' links the compound adjective before the noun, avoiding the misreading 'well known-author'. The apostrophe in 'author's' shows singular possession of the book.
The correct option is: The well-known author's latest book topped the charts.

items.promptText: Which option uses the apostrophe for possession AND the hyphen to clarify meaning?The children's long awaited trip finally arrived.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The childrens long-awaited trip finally arrived.
The childrens' long-awaited trip finally arrived.
The children's long-awaited trip finally arrived.
The children's long awaited trip finally arrived.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The children's long-awaited trip finally arrived.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
'Children's' uses an apostrophe before 's' because 'children' is an irregular plural. 'Long-awaited' needs a hyphen to show the two words work together as one adjective before 'trip'.
The correct option is: The children's long-awaited trip finally arrived.

items.promptText: Which option correctly shows plural possession AND uses a hyphen to avoid misreading?The players hard earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The player's hard-earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
The players hard-earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
The players' hard-earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
The players' hard earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The players' hard-earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
'Players'' has the apostrophe after the 's' because multiple players own the medals (plural possession). 'Hard-earned' needs a hyphen to show it is a single compound adjective.
The correct option is: The players' hard-earned medals were displayed in the cabinet.

items.promptText: Which option uses both the possessive apostrophe AND the hyphen correctly?The school's state of the art science lab impressed visitors.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The schools state-of-the-art science lab impressed visitors.
The school's state of the art science lab impressed visitors.
The school's state-of-the-art science lab impressed visitors.
The schools' state-of-the-art science lab impressed visitors.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The school's state-of-the-art science lab impressed visitors.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
'School's' shows the lab belongs to one school (singular possession). 'State-of-the-art' needs hyphens to form a single compound adjective modifying 'science lab'.
The correct option is: The school's state-of-the-art science lab impressed visitors.

items.promptText: Which option uses the possessive apostrophe AND the hyphen to prevent a misreading?My sister's hand painted vase sits on the shelf.
visibleOptionsOrRows: My sisters hand-painted vase sits on the shelf.
My sister's hand-painted vase sits on the shelf.
My sisters' hand-painted vase sits on the shelf.
My sister's hand painted vase sits on the shelf.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: My sister's hand-painted vase sits on the shelf.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
'Sister's' shows the vase belongs to one sister. 'Hand-painted' needs a hyphen so the reader understands the vase was painted by hand, not that a hand was painted on the vase.
The correct option is: My sister's hand-painted vase sits on the shelf.

items.promptText: Which option uses both possession and hyphenation correctly?The teachers well prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
visibleOptionsOrRows: The teachers' well prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
The teachers well-prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
The teacher's well-prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
The teacher's well prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
items.expectedAnswerSummary: The teacher's well-prepared lesson plan impressed the inspector.
solutionLines: This question tests apostrophes possession and hyphen ambiguity together.
'Teacher's' shows one teacher owns the plan (singular possession). 'Well-prepared' needs a hyphen because the two words form a single adjective before 'lesson plan'.
